title: Unique Paths
problem_statement: There is a robot on an m x n grid. The robot is initially located at the top-left corner (i.e., grid[0][0]). The robot tries to move to the bottom-right corner (i.e., grid[m - 1][n - 1]). The robot can only move either down or right at any point in time. Given the two integers m and n, return the number of possible unique paths that the robot can take to reach the bottom-right corner. The test cases are generated so that the answer will be less than or equal to 2 * 10^9.

examples.explanation:
From the top-left corner, there are a total of 3 ways to reach the bottom-right corner:
1. Right -> Down -> Down
2. Down -> Down -> Right
3. Down -> Right -> Down

intuition: This is a combinatorial problem: to go from (0,0) to (m-1,n-1), you need (m-1) down moves and (n-1) right moves, total paths = C((m-1)+(n-1), m-1). But we can solve it with DP.

approaches.name: Recursive Brute Force
approaches.description: Recurse from (i,j). If at bottom-right, return 1. Else, sum paths from down and right. Base: if out of bounds, 0.
approaches.why_not: Exponential due to recomputing paths to the same cell. Overlapping subproblems.
interview_commentary: This is the straightforward recursive approach. From each cell, try moving down or right, and sum the paths from there.
The problem is that the same cell is reached in many ways, leading to exponential time.
This works for small m,n but not for 100x100.

approaches.name: Memoized Recursion (Top-Down DP)
approaches.description: Same recursion, but memoize on (i,j) to store number of paths from that cell.
approaches.why_not: Bottom-up is iterative and can be done in O(n) space.
interview_commentary: To optimize, I'll memoize on the position (i,j).
This ensures each cell is computed once, time O(m*n).
Space O(m*n) for the memo table.

approaches.name: Bottom-Up Dynamic Programming
approaches.description: Use a 2D dp table where dp[i][j] is paths to (i,j). First row and column = 1. For others, dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j-1].
approaches.why_not: Space can be optimized to O(n) with a 1D array.
interview_commentary: Bottom-up: initialize the first row and column to 1, since there's only one way to reach them (all right or all down).
For each cell, the number of ways is the sum from above and from left.

interview_qa.q: Why is the first row and column 1?
interview_qa.a: Because to reach any cell in the first row, you can only come from the left (all right moves). Similarly for the first column (all down moves).
Use a 1D array of size n, updating it row by row: for each row, for j from 1 to n-1, dp[j] += dp[j-1].
Total moves: (m-1) down + (n-1) right. Number of paths: C((m-1)+(n-1), m-1).
That's Unique Paths II, where you set dp[i][j] = 0 if obstacle, else same.
